coredata.dc:title: Coal derived carbon dots: A versatile tool for Fe3+ sensing, drug detection and nano seed priming

coredata.dc:description: Blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized from low rank coal via a facile ultrasonication method. The synthesized CDs are characterized using various analytical techniques, including UV–vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM analysis revealed an average particle size of 4.3 nm, with a size distribution ranging from 1 to 8 nm. The CDs exhibited strong fluorescence (FL) with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission peak at 453 nm, achieving a quantum yield (QY) of 40.6 %. PL measurements showed a FL decay lifetime of 9.26 ns. In addition, CDs are synthesized, exhibiting excellent stability under UV irradiation, various pH levels, and different solvents, while maintaining consistent PL. The CDs demonstrated effective sensing of Fe3+ ions, with a limit of detection (LoD) of 0.204 μM in a linear response range of 5–50 μM, indicating their potential application in environmental monitoring and bioimaging. Further, CDs are utilized for the detection of tetracycline (TC), achieving a LoD of 0.385 μM within a linear range of 5–50 μM. Nano priming of wheat seeds with CDs at 750 ppm significantly improved growth metrics: average root length (26.7 cm), shoot length (25.2 cm), germination percentage (GP, 100 %), germination index (GI, 531.0), Vigor index-I (4990), seed Vigor-II (4475), and germination energy (GE, 84 %). It also reduced percentage inhibition of growth (PIG, 42.8 %) and mean germination time (MGT, 1 day). These results demonstrate the effectiveness of CDs in enhancing seed germination and seedling development, highlighting their potential as a nano priming agent in agriculture. The application of spray and powder dusting techniques makes it easier to identify latent fingerprints (LFPs) on a variety of substrates, revealing minute level III details like sweat pores and scars. Comprehensive analysis allows for reliable identification even in cases of partial LFPs. The study demonstrates that CDs are effective in Fe3+ sensing, tetracycline detection, enhancing seed germination and LFP visualization, making them promising for wide applications. The findings support the potential of CDs as a versatile nano priming agent to improve natural seed germination and early plant growth and also suggesting their efficacy in criminal investigation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized from low rank coal via a facile ultrasonication method. The synthesized CDs are characterized using various analytical techniques, including UV–vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM analysis revealed an average particle size of 4.3 nm, with a size distribution ranging from 1 to 8 nm. The CDs exhibited strong fluorescence (FL) with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission peak at 453 nm, achieving a quantum yield (QY) of 40.6 %. PL measurements showed a FL decay lifetime of 9.26 ns. In addition, CDs are synthesized, exhibiting excellent stability under UV irradiation, various pH levels, and different solvents, while maintaining consistent PL. The CDs demonstrated effective sensing of Fe3+ ions, with a limit of detection (LoD) of 0.204 μM in a linear response range of 5–50 μM, indicating their potential application in environmental monitoring and bioimaging. Further, CDs are utilized for the detection of tetracycline (TC), achieving a LoD of 0.385 μM within a linear range of 5–50 μM. Nano priming of wheat seeds with CDs at 750 ppm significantly improved growth metrics: average root length (26.7 cm), shoot length (25.2 cm), germination percentage (GP, 100 %), germination index (GI, 531.0), Vigor index-I (4990), seed Vigor-II (4475), and germination energy (GE, 84 %). It also reduced percentage inhibition of growth (PIG, 42.8 %) and mean germination time (MGT, 1 day). These results demonstrate the effectiveness of CDs in enhancing seed germination and seedling development, highlighting their potential as a nano priming agent in agriculture. The application of spray and powder dusting techniques makes it easier to identify latent fingerprints (LFPs) on a variety of substrates, revealing minute level III details like sweat pores and scars. Comprehensive analysis allows for reliable identification even in cases of partial LFPs. The study demonstrates that CDs are effective in Fe3+ sensing, tetracycline detection, enhancing seed germination and LFP visualization, making them promising for wide applications. The findings support the potential of CDs as a versatile nano priming agent to improve natural seed germination and early plant growth and also suggesting their efficacy in criminal investigation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Low-rank coal, representing about 83.91 million tonnes of India's coal resources, is abundant and cost-effective, making it suitable for widespread use [1]. However, its current reliance on combustion power generation leads to resource waste and significant environmental pollution. A more efficient method for utilizing low-rank coal is converting it into high-value carbon materials aligned with its structural features. Coal structure consists of a complex arrangement of sp2 and sp3 carbon domains. Over the past decade, more than 100 models for various coal types have been developed, broadening the possibilities for carbon material production. Higher rank coals, resembling graphite, contain fewer impurities and have simpler processing due to a greater proportion of sp2 structures, which improve stability and decrease reactivity. They are commonly used to create materials like activated carbon and conductive carbon black [2]. In contrast, low-rank coal exhibits a more intricate structure characterized by bonds of sp2 and sp3 atoms, lower coalification levels, reduced calorific value, higher moisture content, and greater volatility. However, its higher hydrogen and oxygen content, along with increased reactivity and well-developed primary pores, enhance its suitability for oxidation reactions [3].

Oxidation processes for treating low-rank coal have become a key research focus in coal utilization. This approach enhances the reactivity of low-rank coal, improving energy efficiency and allowing for the selective production of valuable compounds by fine-tuning reaction parameters. For example, Miura developed a method for oxidizing lignite under mild conditions, achieving high yields of small-molecule fatty acids such as malonic, glycolic, formic, and acetic acids [4]. The proposed oxidation mechanism breaks weaker CO bonds in lignite, forming water-soluble macromolecules and CO2, which further oxidize into small fatty acids. Additionally, the cleavage of certain aromatic rings also contributes to acid generation. Beyond isolating small molecules, strategically breaking weak bonds while maintaining stable crystalline carbon domains presents another significant avenue for utilizing coal. Hu et al. [5] demonstrated the synthesis of carbon nanodots using H2O2 and ultrasonication, achieving impressive FL properties. Similarly, Das et al. [6] employed an ultrasonic-assisted wet chemical method to process low-rank coal into CDs for detecting Ag+ ions, highlighting potential applications in sensing hazardous elements. Adding alkali enhances coal depolymerization. Lu et al. [7] studied density functional theory calculations indicate that alkali can lower the reaction energy of polycyclic groups, promoting side chain and bridge bond oxidation while facilitating ring-opening reactions. Wang et al. [8] found that coal can be more completely oxidized using oxygen or air in alkaline aqueous solutions, resulting in higher yields of benzene polycarboxylic acid. Meanwhile, Bai et al. [9] introduced alkali during oxidation to modify the morphology of carbon dots, achieving significant advancements in solar energy hydrogen production. The potential to improve the depolymerization of low-rank coals and efficiently produce carbon nanoparticles through alkali integration in the oxidation process is noteworthy. Furthermore, studies indicate that under hydrothermal conditions, oxygen-containing functional groups in coal are initially broken down, while stable graphitic structures are preserved, leading to improved coal quality. Table 1 presents a list of coal-derived CDs detailing the various techniques used for their synthesis and applications [10–19].

CDs are quasi-spherical particles less than 10 nm in size that have gained attention in recent scientific reports due to their unique properties, including hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and PL. They hold potential for optical and electrical applications in critical fields, particularly energy and bioengineering [20,21]. The PL properties of CDs stem from surface groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl, which are attached to a nanocarbon core and can be modified through doping with heteroatoms like nitrogen [22]. These modified CDs are highly sensitive to ferric ions, which significantly reduce their PL intensity by forming strong interactions with the functional groups. While CDs can be synthesized from various small organic molecules like glucose, urea, and organic acids, there is substantial potential for producing them from inexpensive, green precursors such as biomass or biomass extracts rich in carbohydrates and natural organic compounds [23–25].

All living organisms require Fe3+ ions, as they play a vital role in metabolic processes such as molecular oxygen transport, nucleic acid synthesis, and electron transport. Additionally, living cells need these ions during infections to maintain their function, metabolism, and proliferation. However, excessive accumulation of Fe3+ beyond physiological limits can adversely affect cellular structures and functions in organs such as the heart, pancreas, kidneys, and liver. Iron overload also impacts bone marrow function and is linked to cancer. While Fe3+ accumulation in humans is primarily associated with contaminated food products, its origins can be traced to the leaching of iron from weathered iron silicates and other minerals into aquatic environments as hydrated iron oxide. Since ferric ions are toxic to fish and other aquatic organisms [26–28], developing rapid monitoring techniques for their detection, even at low concentrations in natural waters, is essential. Among existing methods such as spectrophotometry, flow-injection analysis, graphite furnace atomic absorption, and high-resolution electrospray ionization mass spectrometry the fluorescent method is the most suitable due to its rapidity, high sensitivity, selectivity and lack of tedious pre-treatment requirements. While various “turn-on” fluorescent probes for Fe3+ are available, recent studies have also highlighted the use of CDs as “turn-off” sensors for several metal ions, including Fe3+ [29,30].

CDs can be utilized for energy conversion, bioimaging, optoelectronics, and catalysis. However, significant challenges remain in their development. Recently, various CDs have been synthesized for agricultural applications, serving as sensors, insecticides, fertilizers, and growth regulators to enhance plant quality and productivity. The innovative technique of seed nano-priming uses CDs as nano priming agents to improve seed germination, seedling growth, and stress resistance before sowing. This method may offer a sustainable agronomic practice, as it requires significant amounts of CDs for growth media or foliar sprays throughout the plant life cycle. Unlike prolonged CD treatments, nano-priming chemicals are only applied pre-sowing, resulting in minimal internalization by the plant. Theoretically, employing a nano priming method could minimize the environmental exposure to CDs, particularly for humans and animals that consume plants. Among various nanomaterials, metal and metal oxide nanoparticles are the most commonly used for seed nano-priming. For example, the germination percentage and seedling Vigor index of aged maize seeds can increase threefold when green-synthesized gold NPs are used as a nano-priming agent. Nevertheless, the use of CDs as a nano-priming agent remains uncommon, and research on the mechanisms underlying the interactions between CDs and plants across various CD types and plant species is still limited [31,32]. Table S1 shows the literature survey for seed germination using various carbon dots [33–44].

Fingerprint analysis has been a dependable source of evidence for criminal investigations for more than a century [45]. The ridge patterns from eccrine sweat and greasy sebum are imprinted on solid surfaces by the touch of a finger. The purpose of fingerprint detection is to make this imprint known as a latent fingerprint (LFP) more prominent because it is not visible to the unaided eye. By taking advantage of the chemical or physical interactions that occur between reagents and fingerprint residues, several techniques have been devised to do this. These methods include vacuum metal deposition, cyanoacrylate/iodine fuming, fluorescent dye staining, and powder dusting [46]. The most popular of these techniques, fingerprint powder, sticks to the elements of perspiration and oil on fingertips, but it can also contaminate patterns and erase important data. Because of its high sensitivity, FL staining is a commonly used method for identifying LFPs [47]. Fluorescent dyes, on the other hand, usually emit light at a single wavelength, are poisonous, and are expensive. CQDs have recently attracted a lot of attention for fingerprinting applications as a solution to these problems. Their diminutive dimensions facilitate precise color reproduction and adjustability, rendering them a propitious substitute for crafting more efficacious fingerprinting methodologies. Fluorescent CDs have garnered significant attention over the past decade due to their cost-effectiveness, eco-friendly synthesis, stable luminescence, and favorable biosafety and environmental properties. Although CDs are widely employed in drug administration, bioimaging, FL sensing, and other analyses, there aren't many publications on their usage in LFP detection [48].

In this study, we propose utilizing waste coal for the preparation of CDs via ultrasonication method. The prepared CDs are characterized by various techniques, including XRD, FE-SEM, EDS, TEM, FT-IR, Raman and FL spectral analysis to understand their structural properties, surface morphology, elemental composition, functional groups, crystallinity and optical characteristics. Additionally, CDs exploring their potential as a fluorescent “turn-off” sensor for Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, we aim to extend the applicability of these CDs by examining solvent test, pH test and salt resistant test and additionally highlighting their significance in TC drug detection. Furthermore, nano-priming through wheat seed germination using CDs has paved the way for diverse agricultural applications. In addition, the blue-emissive fluorescent CDs was employed for spraying and powder dusting techniques to visualize LFPs. We successfully addressed the common aggregation-induced FL quenching of CDs, and the blue emission helps limit background FL interference. We used a variety of everyday surfaces to demonstrate how FPs form and pattern, and we were able to precisely capture the LFPs under an UV light source right after dusting or spraying. Thus, this research not only addresses the environmental challenges associated with coal waste but also offers innovative solutions for monitoring criminal investigations and enhancing agricultural practises.

Sub-Bituminous coal samples are sourced from the North-Eastern Coalfields (Coal India Limited), India. Approximately 1.5 kg of the raw coal was ground to a fineness of ∼0.211 mm using a standard method (ASTM 2010). Sigma Aldrich provided the following chemicals, all of which are 99.9 % pure: hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), ammonium solution, ethanol, ascorbic acid (AA), ZnCl2, CaCl2, CrCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, AgCl, CoCl2, PbCl2, CuCl2, AlCl3, MnCl2, NaCl, CdCl2, dexamethasone, aspirin, tetracycline, acetaminophen, ofloxacin, trimethoprim, vancomycin, 2-nitrofuran, azithromycin, and dithizone. Furthermore, aqueous solution was prepared using deionized (DI) water and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA: molecular weight 1500). For product purification, fresh 0.22-μm polytetrafluoroethylene membranes and dialysis bags (Sigma Aldrich) are employed. The wheat grains are purchased at a Tumkur local market, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sonochemical synthesis of CDs from low-rank coal
ce:section.ce:para: Under ice-cold conditions, 200 mL of 30 % H2O2 was combined with 12 g of coal samples. After that, the reaction mixture was subjected to ultrasonication (frequency: 40 kHz) for approximately 6 h at room temperature and atmospheric pressure using an ultrasonicator controlled by a microprocessor. The mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature before ammonium solution was added to bring the pH level to neutral. After passing the mixture through a 0.22-μm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane, 1 kDa dialysis bags are used to further purify it. To acquire the purified CDs, the fluid was condensed using a rotary evaporator following dialysis. The resultant CDs are lyophilized to eliminate any remaining moisture, and the powder was then kept at room temperature for further examination and LFP visualization. Fig. 1 a provides a schematic representation of the CDs synthesis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs ion sensing ability for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To test the selectivity of the CDs solution towards various metal ions at room temperature, a mass concentration of 0.5 mg/mL was used. Twelve stock solutions (5 μM each) of metal ions from their respective salts, including Zn2+, Ca2+, Cr2+, Ag+, Co2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Na+ and Cd2+, are prepared independently. Typically, 1 mL of each metal salt solution was carefully mixed with 3 mL of the CDs solution. Each test was meticulously conducted, and the accompanying FL spectra are collected after the reactions are allowed to proceed at room temperature for 10 min. A solution containing 3 mL of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) and varied amounts of Fe3+ (5–50 μM) are added to determine the sensitivity of CDs to Fe3+. At 365 nm, the excitation wavelength was adjusted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity of prepared CDs to detect tetracycline (TC)
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CDs are combined with stock solutions (5 μM) of different drugs and compounds, such as dithizone, acetaminophen, tetracycline, ofloxacin, aspirin, trimethoprim, vancomycin, nitrofuran, azithromycin, and trimethoprim, to assess their selectivity in a consistent environment. TC was added to the CDs (0.5 mg/mL) at different doses to perform FL analysis over the wavelength range of 350–650 nm. In addition, with the concentration of interfering ions fixed at ten times that of TC, the interference impact of these pharmacological compounds was examined in the presence of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigations into wheat germination and seedling development
ce:section.ce:para: To eliminate bacterial contamination, mercuric chloride (HgCl2) was first applied to all of the healthy wheat grains that had been chosen. This sterilization process lasted for 5 min. Rinsing the seeds in DI water for 10–15 min remove any leftover HgCl2. A full 24 h went by allowing fifty seeds to grow in individual petri dishes that had 5 mL of CDs solution in various concentrations. 50 healthy seeds are left to grow in individual petri dishes containing 5 mL of CDs solution at varying concentrations A up to 24 h. The control group consisted of seeds soaked in DI water. The sprouted seeds are then transferred to germination sheets that had been previously soaked in CDs solution at several concentrations (Control, 100, 250, 500, 750, and 1000 ppm). After that, these sheets are securely wrapped and maintained in a typical setting for the duration of the seedling's growth. This configuration is shown schematically in Fig. S1. Each experiment was conducted three times to assess how reliable the system was. Using thread, the seedlings' roots and branches are measured. Following a 10-day incubation period, measurements of several pairs of seminal roots, leaves, and shoots are obtained for study from the germinated seeds.

Wheat seedlings from different CDs doses are randomly selected, and the length and diameter of their roots and shoots are measured using a ruler and a vernier calliper on the 5th and 10th days. After being weighed to ascertain their fresh weight, the corresponding roots and shoots are kept at a consistent 60 °C for 2 days in a drying box. After drying, the samples are weighed again to record their dry weight.

ce:section.ce:para:

Here, the length of the germinated seeds in the CDs and control samples is represented by Lt and Lc, respectively, while the total number of germinated seeds in the CDs and control samples is indicated by Sc and St. The average root length of the seeds in the control and CDs samples is represented by the values Cc and Cd, respectively. The number of seeds that germinated on day d is indicated by the variable n, and the number of days since germination started is shown by the variable d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of chlorophyll content in wheat leaves
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on varying amounts of CDs in the leaves of sprouted wheat in a deionized water system, the chlorophyll content was calculated. Harborne [50] described a method for extracting the photosynthetic pigment content. First, sprouting wheat leaves that are completely green are partially hydrated by immersion in 95 % ethanol. To ensure complete lysis, the leaves are next crushed into a paste and immersed in 85 % ethanol. After 45 min in the dark, the paste was centrifuged for 5 min at 5000 rpm. The solution's absorbance was measured at 470, 645, and 663 nm, and the formulas from Arnon et al. [51] and Lichtenthaler et al. [52] are used to determine the amount of chlorophyll present.

)

Every measurement is done three times, and the mean standard deviation is the output that is shown. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the Tukey test (P < 0.05) are used for the statistical analysis of the experimental data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LFPs collection and recognition via powder dusting method and spray method
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 1b, the process of creating and visualizing LFPs using the powder dusting and spray methods is described in detail. LFPs were obtained from a 25-year-old female donor, who washed and dried her hands with soapy water. After gently rubbing sebum-rich areas like the forehead and nose, she pressed her thumb onto various surfaces, including steel plates, plastic, aluminum foil, and compact discs. The fingerprints on these surfaces were treated by spraying with a solution of carbon dots (CDs). Visualization was conducted with a digital camera under both ambient light and UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm. For the powder dusting technique, CDs powder was applied using a magnetic brush to identify the LFPs. Photos were then captured under UV light at 365 nm.

To reduce the effects of re-absorption, quinine sulfate (QS) reference and CDs are prepared at 5 different concentrations, each with an absorbance value below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The QS is dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 54 %, refractive index: 1.33) to produce the reference solution. By measuring the integrated FL intensities at the same excitation wavelength and contrasting the sample absorbance values with the reference, the QY of the CDs is determined. The following formula is used to estimate the QY of the synthesized CDs [53]:
(11)
QY

where QY represents the FL-QY of the CDs, and φ std denotes the FL-QY of the standard QS. I sample and I std refer to the integrated FL intensities of the CDs and QS, respectively. A sample and A std are the absorbance values of the CDs and the standard QS, while η sample and η std represent the refractive indices of the CDs and the standard QS.

Using the following tools, we used a variety of methods to fully characterize the coal-derived CDs.
Sl No Technique Instrument and specification Purpose
1 XRD A Cu K α radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) diffractometer Shimadzu-7000 is used. Crystalline structure
2 FESEM Hitachi SU-8010 Surface morphology
3 TEM High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) using the JEOL JEM-2100 with the HITACHI LTD H-7500 Determination of particle size
4 EDAX Hitachi SU-8010, FESEM Elemental analysis
5 XPS A PHI 5000 Versa-probe spectrometer with a monochromatic Al K α radiation source. Chemical composition, charge state, and binding
Energy
6 FT-IR Perkin-Elmer spectrophotometer Detecting surface species
7 UV–vis absorbance A JASCO V-670 spectrophotometer Absorbance spectra
8 PL Horiba (Jobin Yvon) spectrofluorometer, with a slit width of 2 nm and a 450 W xenon lamp for excitation. PL and PLE

The crystalline structure of the CDs is examined using the potent non-destructive method of X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD pattern of raw coal is seen in Fig. 2 a. It represents the (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon, with large peaks at roughly 22° and 43°. However, CDs show wide peaks in their XRD spectra at roughly 22.42° and 43.66°. A wider interlayer spacing of 0.408 nm than that of graphite is observed in the (002) lattice of graphitic carbon, which is represented by the peak at 22.42° [54]. The degree of graphitization seen on the surface of CDs was dramatically increased upon the addition of groups containing nitrogen and oxygen.

Using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM), the morphology of the carbon nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 2b and d. As seen in Fig. 2c, elemental composition of the generated CDs was ascertained by means of energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). Carbon (84.61 %) and oxygen (9.73 %) are the two main elements found in the CDs, with nitrogen (5.65 %) making up the remaining percentage. This shows that hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups are among the several functionalizations present on the surface of the generated CDs. The particle size distribution of the CDs under TEM is shown in Fig. 2e. Statistical analysis indicates that the carbon nanoparticles have a uniform size, with an average is estimated to be 4.3 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image in Fig. 2f clearly displays lattice stripes. With lattice spacings of 0.22 nm, which correspond to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, the structures are essentially single-crystal (Fig. 2g). Furthermore, Fig. 2h accurately reports the interplanar distance as 0.21 nm, which was computed using GATAN digital micrograph.

Fig. 3 a displays the Raman spectrum of the prepared CDs. The G band, which indicates the existence of graphitic carbon, correlates to the in-plane vibration of sp3 hybridized carbon, whereas the D band is linked to flaws and faults. Furthermore, the disordered D band (∼1378.72 cm−1) has a D/G ratio of 0.81, which means that it is marginally weaker than the orderly G band (1590.80 cm−1). Given that the ratio is significantly smaller than the D/G ratio of 1.00 found in raw coal, it is likely that the CDs primarily have a rigid graphite structure [55]. In the meantime, surface groups of the CDs as made are characterized by FT-IR studies. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, the peak that emerged at 3413.26 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching modes of OH, the CH vibrations at 2897.97 cm−1, the vibration absorption band of CO in COOH at 1611.60 cm−1, the bending vibrations of CC at 1434.45 cm−1, the bending vibrations of CN at 1160.73 cm−1, and the asymmetric vibrations of CO at 849.52 cm−1. The FTIR spectra showed corresponding peaks for CC and CO functional groups, supporting the UV–vis results. The FTIR measurements clearly showed that functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen have functionalized the CDs. The resulting CDs are incredibly water soluble due to the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups. Furthermore, XPS analyses are carried out to look into the bonding modes and surface elemental properties of the created CDs. Typical peaks for C 1s, N 1s and O 1s may be seen in the survey XPS spectra displayed in Fig. 3c, at ∼280, 390 and 530 eV, respectively. The chemical surroundings of C, N and O elements are elucidated in detail by use of high-resolution spectra. The CC, CC, CO/CN and CO/CN groups are represented by the peaks in the C 1s spectra (Fig. 3d) at 285.30, 286.60, 287.94 and 289.01 eV, respectively. The effective synthesis of CDs is further confirmed by the high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 3e), which show a variety of N species, including graphitic N at 397.94 eV. Deconvoluting the O 1s spectra (Fig. 3f) into two fitted peaks at roughly 531.82 eV and 532.17 eV verified that OCO and CO groups are present on the surface of CDs [56]. This feature significantly improves the CDs water compatibility. In fact, even after six months of storage at room temperature, the supernatant solution is still homogenous and devoid of noticeable precipitation.

The low-rank coal was utilized as the precursor for the one-pot oxidation process to create CDs. Under typical atmospheric circumstances, visible and ultraviolet light are applied to the prepared CDs. The information showed that when exposed to visible light, the CDs had a dark yellowish-brown appearance. On the other hand, when exposed to UV light at λ exci = 365 nm, the particles released a vivid blue hue (inset of Fig. 4 a). The reason for this behavior is that electronic spectra in the UV range are significantly more activated than those in the visible area. The π–π* and n-π* transitions of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms are highlighted when electrons are stimulated from their ground state to an excited state by exposure to UV light. Moreover, two peaks can be seen in the UV–vis spectrum of the produced CDs at 275 nm and 340 nm, respectively (Fig. 4a), which represent the π–π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond [57].

To comprehend the surface state properties of the produced CDs, an analysis was conducted on the PL emission properties. The excitation and emission spectra of CDs with an emission wavelength of 453 nm and an excitation wavelength of 350 nm are displayed in Fig. 4b. Two categories are identified in the FL spectrum of the CDs at various excitation wavelengths: 260 nm to 350 nm and 360 nm to 450 nm (Fig. 4c). The data show that when the excitation wavelength was raised from 260 nm to 350 nm, the CDs FL emission significantly increased and showed a blue shift in peak position. Nevertheless, when the sample was stimulated between 360 and 450 nm, the FL spectra revealed a discernible red shift and a drop in emission intensity. Excitation at 350 nm resulted in the greatest emission for the produced CDs. Furthermore, a 3D contour plot shows the FL spectra at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4d). The unique hues of the FL emission changed from blue to green over the excitation wavelength range of 260 nm to 450 nm, matching coordinates (0.130, 0.178) and (0.186, 0.512), respectively. The developed CDs CIE (International Commission on Illumination) plot (Fig. 4e) shows these variances and the data is provided in Table S2. The produced CDs carbon core states or surface state flaws are mostly responsible for these emission differences. Surface state defects are important for the CDs PL process [58]. Through additional testing, the photo-stability of CDs a crucial component for real-world applications was evaluated. To assess the photo-stability of the CDs, they are subjected to a high-intensity ultraviolet (UV) source (6 mW/cm2) at 365 nm for 120 min. The findings, which are displayed in Fig. 4f, demonstrate that the irradiated CDs have outstanding photo-stability and very slightly alter their FL intensity in comparison to the non-irradiated CDs. Literature review on comparing the FL intensity after UV irradiation in various CDs systems of other CDs with present work is listed in Table S3a [59–64]. In order to study the fluorescence stability of the as-synthesized CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs after storage at room temperature for 90 days and 180 days were tested. As shown in the Fig. S2(a & b), the fluorescence intensity decreased by only 2 % after storage for 90 days and 9.99 % by 180 days. The minimal decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs over 180 days is due to their stable surface passivation and resistance to environmental degradation like oxidation or aggregation. Proper synthesis and storage conditions further preserve their optical properties. These factors make CDs highly stable for long-term applications.

The surface functional groups on the particles have a significant impact on the manufactured CDs optical characteristics. Fig. 4g and h show how QS and CDs solutions are examined using UV–vis absorption and FL spectra in order to calculate the QY. With carbon, oxygen and nitrogen atoms present in greater surface functionalization, the small size of the particles, a high aromatic sp2 domain inside the carbon core, or the quantum confinement effect, the CDs QY of 40.9 % was detected. The FL behavior of CDs was investigated by altering pH, moisture and salt tolerance prior to examining its potential for sensing applications. We carefully considered the effects of pH, moisture content and salt content on the developed CDs. The emission spectra of CDs at different concentrations of acetone 100 %, 80 %, 60 %, 40 %, 20 % and 0 % are shown in Fig. 4i. The findings demonstrated that the particles responded significantly to solvents with high polarity, such as water. When low-polarity organic solvents (acetone) are present, the spectra showed a blue shift and when the water concentration rose, the shift turned red. These findings imply that sophisticated methods are not necessary for the prepared CDs to be utilized as visual probes for detecting tiny amounts of water in organic solvents. Significantly, the CDs FL spectra showed a more noticeable shift, suggesting that this technique is extremely sensitive for moisture detection. The robustness of CDs under varying pH and ionic strength conditions depends on their surface functional groups, synthesis method. In Basic condition (8–14) CDs are generally stable in basic environments due to the presence of functional groups like carboxyl and hydroxyl, which resist degradation under high pH. However, at extremely high pH, the functional groups or bonds in the carbon core may degrade, leading to reduced fluorescence. Acidic Conditions (2–6) CDs with carboxyl or amine groups tend to retain stability in moderately acidic conditions (Fig. 4j). Extreme acidity can protonate surface functional groups, which may alter their optical properties and reduce stability. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4k, the CDs continued to exhibit consistent emission peaks when the concentration of NaCl increased from 0 M to 1 M. These findings validate that the produced CDs have good pH stability and salt tolerance in the pH range of 6 to 10 in ambient circumstances, which makes them appropriate for a variety of uses.

Excitation wavelengths of ∼ 300 nm and 420 nm, which correspond to the short and long emission FL centers of CDs, respectively, are shown in Fig. S3a and b as the emission spectra at different concentrations. Because of the agglomeration of CDs at greater concentrations, concentration had a significant impact on the FL behavior. The particle size and, thus, the energy band structure of the CDs are altered by this aggregation. Under 300 nm excitation, a redshift of ∼48 nm in the emission wavelength was seen with increasing concentration (Fig. S3a). On the other hand, the FL peak underwent modest susceptibility to concentration fluctuations when exposed to long-wavelength illumination (420 nm), remaining constant ∼513 nm (Fig. S3b). This implies that whereas FL centers activated by longer wavelengths might be linked to defects or functional groups inside the CDs, those excited by shorter wavelengths are probably impacted by variations in particle size. The relationship between FL intensity and carbon dot concentration at various excitation wavelengths is displayed in Fig. S3c. It was found that the CDs FL intensity progressively reduced as concentration increased at lower excitation wavelengths (300 nm), most likely as a result of particle aggregation at higher concentrations. On the other hand, the FL intensity increased at higher excitation wavelengths (420 nm) and then gradually decreased. The increase in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups that occurs when CDs are concentrated can be used to explain this pattern. Initially, this raises surface states or adds electronic states, which improves FL. But an overabundance of functional groups probably results in non-radiative recombination centers and changes the CDs electronic structure, which lowers FL efficiency in the end [65,66].

Both short- and long-wavelength emissions are responsible for the overall FL behavior of CDs. The fundamental makeup of CDs and the related luminescence processes are shown in Fig. S4. Clusters exhibiting FL at shorter emission wavelengths, which correspond to emissions from the core state of the sp2 structural domain, are highlighted in the blue region. Higher-energy light is released in this domain due to the conjugated structure formed by π electrons and the amplification of π → π transitions by the quantum size effect. Intrinsic FL, particularly blue FL (∼448 nm), is produced by this mechanism. The interplay of surface imperfections resulting from oxygen atoms and the impacts of functional groups within CDs is responsible for the longer emission wavelength FL observed in the pink region. These flaws produce green FL (∼505 nm) through photo-induced FL caused by the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs at these defect sites, as organic fluorophores do [67].

Fe3+ detection in aqueous solutions was assessed using the as-prepared CDs as sensors. Using a 0.5 mg/mL solution of CDs, a selectivity investigation was first carried out due to the difficulty of detecting metal ions in actual environmental circumstances. An array of metal ions are introduced one at a time, with concentrations of 5 μM for Zn2+, Ca2+, Cr2+, Ag+, Co2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Na+ and Cd2+. As can be seen in Fig. 5 a, the addition of the other metal ions had no effect on the FL intensity of the CDs, however only in the presence of Fe3+ would it dramatically decrease. Uniform particle size distribution reduces variability in fluorescence response, contributing to consistent optical behavior. The functional groups on the CDs interact specifically with Fe3+ ions, increasing binding affinity and leading to a pronounced quenching effect [68]. The low background fluorescence of the CDs further enhances sensitivity, allowing for accurate detection at lower ion concentrations. Additionally, the high surface area of the CDs improves their accessibility to Fe3+ ions, boosting detection precision [69]. These factors collectively improve the fluorescence response, making the method highly effective. The combination of quantum size effects, strong interactions with Fe3+, and low background fluorescence makes the CDs a reliable tool for trace detection.

The quenching effect might be attributed to energy transfer between the d-orbitals of metal atoms and the surface groups of CDs, or it could be the result of a chelating complex formed between the surface functional groups of CDs and Fe3+ ions. The strongly quenched Fe3+ ions are shown by the very low F/F0 values. A selectivity investigation is also carried out to evaluate possible interference from co-existing ions. To assess cross-reactivity, CDs are added to a mixed solution including Fe3+ and other metal ions, as seen in Fig. 5b. The signal interference is ∼ 10 % when Fe3+ at the same concentration is combined with co-existing metal ions. This outcome validated the suggested method's exceptional Fe3+ detection selectivity. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5c, experiments using CDs in isolation (without Fe3+) and at different Fe3+ concentrations (5–50 μM) showed that the CDs are efficiently quenched by Fe3+ ions. As seen in Fig. 5d, the correlation coefficient (R2) is ∼0.9973 within the 5–50 μM concentration range, showing a strong association. The Sterne-Volmer calibration curve, which plots the ratio of F/F0 against the concentration of the specific metal ion, is constructed using the following formula [70].
(13)

where F 0 is the emission intensity of CDs in the absence of metal ions, F is the emission intensity of CDs in the presence of metal ions, Q is the concentration and τ is the lifespan of the excited state. The following formulas are also used to establish the LoD and limit of quantification (LoQ) by optimizing the integrated area under the peak [71].

SD denotes the standard deviation. The concentration curve for Fe3+ ions showed a positive slope, with a LoD of 0.204 μM and LoQ of 0.681 μM. This demonstrates significant energy transfer and a high absorption affinity between CDs and ferric ions. Table 2 [72–80] shows that this method has a much lower detection limit than previously published strategies for Fe3+ detection. Because of their sensitivity and selectivity, CDs may be useful for monitoring ferric ion levels in contaminated waterways.

Fig. 5e depicts the quenching and re-quenching behaviors of CDs. Adding 10 μM Fe3+ to the CDs solution significantly reduces FL intensity. However, adding Ascorbic Acid (AA) to the combination restores the FL. AA and Fe3+ interact strongly, causing loosely bound Fe3+ to be released from the surface of CDs. Fig. 5f illustrates the fluorescence quenching and restoration process involving Fe3+ and AA. Fluorescence quenching occurs due to complexation-induced aggregation, facilitated by excited-state electron transfer from CDs to Fe3+. The subsequent recovery of emission intensity is driven by the redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA. A molecular logic gate is designed as a result of the effects of various chemicals, including Fe3+ and AA, on the FL spectra of CDs. We used the simple chemistry of the Fenton reaction where Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+ in the presence of H2O2 to develop a multiple-input, single-output logic gate. This reaction is represented by the following equation,
(16) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + OH▪

Using this knowledge, we conducted a thorough investigation of the impacts of Fe2+ on the emission signal of CDs, as well as the combined effects of Fe2+ + H2O2 and Fe2+ + H2O2 + AA. An input signal of 1 indicates the presence of an analyte, while a signal of 0 indicates its absence. The FL state is reflected in the output signal. In this design, a normal FL intensity represents a value of 1, while a quenched FL signifies 0. A single-input NOT gate is created with Fe3+ as the sole input and the FL intensity of CDs serves as the output (Fig. S5a & a1). When Fe3+ is present (input ‘1’), it results in a logical negation from “1” to “0”; conversely, in the absence of Fe3+ (input ‘0’), the output is “1.” Given that the addition of AA can restore the FL in the CDs-Fe3+ system, a two-input logic gate is constructed with Fe3+ as input 1 and AA as input 2 (Fig. S5b & b1). In this configuration, the presence of Fe3+ alone (input ‘1’) results in a low output (output ‘0’), while the presence of AA (input ‘1’) restores the FL, leading to a high output (output ‘1’). Additionally, another molecular logic gate is designed with Fe2+ and H2O2 as the two inputs, providing insight into the oxidation process of Fe2+ to Fe3+. The FL of CDs is stable when either Fe3+ or H2O2 is present alone, but when both are present, there is a notable quenching of the FL because of the production of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S5c & c1, a NAND gate is built using these outputs. Furthermore, Fe3+ is utilized as the first input, Fe3+ as the second input and H2O2 as the third input in the design of a triple-input logic gate (Fig. S5d & d1). With this configuration, it is possible to distinguish clearly between Fe3+ and Fe2+ because the FL probe is highly responsive to Fe3+ and less sensitive to Fe2+. Finally, Fe3+, AA, Fe3+ and H2O2 are designed as the first, second, third and fourth inputs, respectively, of a four-input logic gate (Fig. S5e & e1). Using the multi-input logic gate can help with the creation of smart molecular devices based on FL.

We investigated the possible mechanism of interaction between CDs and Fe3+. When Fe3+ was added as a quencher, the absorbance of the CDs showed a minor shift, as shown in Fig. 5g. Nonetheless, the absorption peak's position did not change, indicating that Fe3+ did not affect the CDs structure and supporting the dynamic quenching mechanism. Additionally, Fig. 5h confirms that dynamic quenching is responsible for the fluorescence quenching at Fe3+ concentrations in the linear range (0–50 μM). Collisions between Fe3+ ions and CDs cause this dynamic quenching, which lowers the intensity of the fluorescence. Therefore, dynamic quenching is the main mechanism governing fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ in the linear detection range (0–50 μM).

The decay lifetime spectrum of CDs and CDs with addition of Fe3+ given in Fig. 5i, is best fitted with tri-exponential function corresponding to Y(t) = A 1exp(−t/τ 1) + A 2exp(−t/τ 2) + A 3exp (−t/τ 3). where, Y(t) is normalized PL intensity as a function of time; τ 1, τ 2 and τ 3, are the time resolved decay lifetime of three radiative decay channels, A 1, A 2 and A 3 are the corresponding amplitudes. The decay lifetimes and their corresponding amplitudes are listed in the inset of Fig. 5i. The average lifetime of CDs is calculated as 9.26 and 7.75 ns, obtained using the following expression [65].

The tri-exponential behavior of CDs implies presence of three types of emissive centres that correspond to the internal carbon core and external surface traps. The shorter lifetime component is attributable to the direct e-h recombination, whereas the relatively longer lifetime are likely to be due to emission from defect states of CDs.

Therefore,

〉
= 7.75 ns.

As can be seen from the Fig. 5i, after the addition of 50 μM Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs dropped dramatically from 9.26 ns to 7.75 ns, further demonstrating that fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ is a dynamic quenching process [81]. Dynamic quenching, as supported by our fluorescence lifetime measurements and absorption studies, involves collisional interactions between Fe3+ and CDs, leading to a reduction in fluorescence intensity without altering the CDs structure. This mechanism inherently involves interactions in the excited state, which were initially attributed to electron transfer. However, further analysis, including the unchanged absorption peak positions and the significant decrease in fluorescence lifetime, strongly indicates that the quenching is better described as dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing capability of prepared CDs towards tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CDs are excellent candidates for biosensing applications because of their FL characteristics. Using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the incubation period for the analysis is set at 2 min. Selectivity is evaluated by measuring the emission intensity of CDs in the presence of different drug molecules, each at a concentration of 5 μM, such as dithizone, vancomycin, nitrofuran, azithromycin, acetaminophen, aspirin, tetracycline, ofloxacin, trimethoprim and dexamethasone. TC elicited a significant fluorometric reaction with the CDs, but the other analytes only yielded marginal responses (Fig. 6 a). The synthesized sensor's dynamic performance is assessed at TC concentrations ranging from 5 μM to 50 μM (Fig. 6b). The interaction between the functional groups on the CDs and the TC is directly correlated with the FL intensity, which consistently decreased with increasing TC concentration. This indicates that the strong FL response to TC is attributed to the high number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which facilitate electron transfer between the two molecules. Additionally, an increase in TC concentration results in a shift of the FL peak from 456 nm to 512 nm. This shift occurs because TC alone emits a weak FL signal at 512 nm, which, when interacting with CDs, shifts the FL spectra to a higher wavelength. These results suggest that the CDs are highly sensitive to TC, likely due to the molecule's interaction and immobilization within the CDs.

The ratio F0/F is computed, where F0 and F stand for FL emission of CDs while TC is present and absent, respectively. As the concentration of TC increased, the FL results demonstrated that this might lead to an increase in the F0/F value (Fig. 6c) and a considerable change in the color of CDs (see the inset of Fig. 6c). Typically, sensors are used to study their practical applications using indices such as linear range, LoD and LoQ. A linear connection with an R2 value of 0.993 is discovered between the TC concentration and intensity ratio between 5 μM and 50 μM. The LoD and LoQ are calculated using Eqs. (13)–(15), resulting in a LoD of 0.385 μM and a LoQ of 0.983 μM. Selectivity is a crucial factor in evaluating the sensing capability of the prepared fluorophore towards quenchers. The selectivity of the nanoprobe is assessed with various analogues, including drugs and other organic compounds. It is observed that CDs specifically detect the TC drug, showing substantial quenching of FL emission spectra compared to other drug molecules. Consequently, it is important to further investigate the sensing potential of CDs for TC in the presence of other interfering ions. Therefore, various analogues, including vancomycin, nitrofuran, azithromycin, dithizone, ofloxcin, acetaminophen, dexamethasone and trimethoprim, are evaluated directly to assess their effect on CDs quenching (Fig. 6d). The findings showed that these analytes had no effect on the TC detection process. This illustrates the remarkable anti-interference capacity of the nano-sensor and shows that it can detect TC specifically. The exceptional selectivity of the produced CDs for TC is confirmed by the FL spectra, which remained outstanding even in the presence of increased concentrations of interfering species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Seed germination assay in presence of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Edible food plants are essential to the Earth's food chain and play a major part in the ecology. We have selected ``wheat,'' a widely utilized edible seed, for our investigation. Five different concentrations of CDs, ranging from 100 to 1000 ppm, are applied in order to investigate their effects on wheat seed growth. Seedlings developed from seeds treated with the control sample and those treated with the CDs solution showed notable phenotypic variations. Fig. 7 shows the growth of seedlings at different times (5th and 10th days) following different seed priming treatments (control and 100–1000 ppm). The low phytotoxicity of CDs in this study is demonstrated by the consistent superior performance of seedlings treated with high doses of CDs (750 ppm) over those from the control group in terms of the observed phenotypes. The produced CDs, however, might still have an effect on plant growth, especially on metabolic functions. The CDs concentration, size and physicochemical characteristics all have a big impact on how quickly seeds germinate. The features of the selected CDs have been characterized by their size, shape and surface functionalization. The phytotoxicity shown in seeds that are germinating and the CDs surface area and particular surface features are significantly correlated. Plant cell growth is further supported by the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. But germination also depends on other elements, such the characteristics of the seeds themselves. Mature dried seeds, for example, can speed up cellular metabolism and growth because they absorb water more effectively. The growth of rootlets is also influenced by the kind of micronuclei found in the seeds [82].

Large doses of CDs do not significantly improve plant development, according to studies [83,84]. Elevated levels of CDs have the potential to impair the physiological processes of wheat seeds, possibly as a result of heightened generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and oxidative stress [85]. High concentrations of CDs can build up on seed coats, which makes it difficult for the seed to absorb water, delaying seed germination and seedling growth [86]. In contrast, wheat seedling development indices improved when the dosage of CDs is increased from 100 to 750 ppm. It is thought that CDs interact favourably with seeds at lower concentrations, promoting overall growth. According to the findings, 750 ppm is the optimal dose for promoting seed germination and seedling growth. To better understand the effects of CDs priming, future research will look at the biochemical indicators of wheat seeds and seedlings, with a special emphasis on the group that received the optimal dose of CDs and the control group. A number of parameters, including average root length, shoot length, germination percentage (GP), germination index (GI), percentage inhibition of growth (PIG), vigor index-I (VI-I), seed vigor-II (SV-II), germination energy (GE) and mean germination time (MGT), will be measured in order to better understand how CDs affect germination ability (GA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of average root and shoot length, diameter and biomass in presence of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
With higher seed inhibition and germination rates, an increase in seedling growth and biomass buildup is anticipated to be advantageous. In order to corroborate this, measurements of the length, diameter, and biomass of the seedlings are made on the 5th and 10th following harvest, as shown in Figs. 8 and 9
. The findings show that the effects of CDs on seedling growth are dose-dependent, with the control group underperforming the CDs treated groups, which had concentrations between 100 and 1000 ppm. Fig. 8a illustrates how seedling shoot and root lengths increased up to a CDs concentration of 750 ppm before substantially declining when concentrations increased to 1000 ppm. The seedlings treated with 750 ppm of CDs demonstrated a maximum shoot and root lengths of 25.2 cm and 26.7 cm respectively. An increase in shoot and root length is closely linked to overall plant health and productivity. Longer shoots enhance photosynthesis by providing a larger surface area for light absorption, boosting energy production [87]. This supports higher growth rates and metabolic activities. Longer roots improve the plant's ability to absorb water and nutrients, essential for maintaining healthy cellular functions [88]. Additionally, well-developed roots provide stability, helping the plant resist environmental stressors and improve disease resistance. The larger root diameter could improve both mechanical stability and water absorption efficiency. A thicker root system, even without soil anchorage, can help the seedlings better stabilize themselves on the germination sheet by increasing surface contact with the substrate. This enhanced root diameter likely improves the seedling's ability to absorb water from the sheet more efficiently, allowing for better hydration and nutrient uptake through the contact points with the wet surface [89]. Overall, increased shoot and root length leads to better resource allocation, promoting higher reproductive output and increased productivity.

Thus 750 ppm of CDs treated group showed a significant increase in both shoot (67 %, P < 0.001) and root (69 %, P < 0.001) lengths when compared to the control group. Furthermore, in comparison to the control group, the treatment with 750 ppm CDs led to a 55 % increase in shoot diameter and a 61 % rise in root diameter (Fig. 8b). The maximum attained root and shoot diameter are found to be 0.37 mm and 0.76 mm respectively, indicating that a stronger root system is essential for encouraging seedling growth. This suggests that CDs priming could promote the growth of a more resilient root system. Furthermore, during the sprouting phase, wheat sprouts treated with CDs showed more split roots (Fig. 8e). This feature emphasizes a major advantage that is frequently missed in earlier research on the use of CDs to stimulate plant growth. As can be seen in Fig. 8c, which shows the fresh biomass of the shoot and root, the group treated with 750 ppm of CDs accumulated more biomass (248.98 mg of shoot and 35.75 mg of root fresh weight) than the control group. Following seed priming with the 750 ppm CDs solution, the fresh biomass grew by 55 % for shoots and 43 % for roots while dry biomass rose by 53 % for roots and 65 % for shoots in comparison to the control (Fig. 8d). The detailed measurements are provided in Table 3 (a–d). These findings suggest that priming with CDS can improve the accumulation of biomass in seedlings, which is essential for seedling growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on seed germination, germination index and percentage inhibition growth
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of the toxicity of seed germination when produced CDs are present has shown the benefits of the suggested approach and its potential to advance agricultural environmental applications. It is evident that the type and quality of the seeds utilized affects the biocompatibility and non-toxic properties of CDs in addition to their composition and concentration. The higher Germination Energy of 0.84 shows that the produced CDs have greatly enhanced the seeds capacity to germinate swiftly and uniformly. As seeds germinate, they become metabolically active and respire rapidly in order to transform food into energy. Fig. 8f illustrates how the germination energy of wheat seeds increased from 100 to 750 ppm of CDs and how the average time needed for germination decreased from 2.2 days to 1 day as CDs concentrations rose to the ideal level. The germination ability held consistent after 10 days of harvesting for all CDs concentrations. Additional research on the higher productivity of plants grown in ideal conditions showed that, as shown in Fig. 9a, the germination index of seedlings grew linearly with increasing concentrations of CDs. Additionally, it is noted that the chosen CDs do not obstruct seeds aquaporins, or transmembrane water channels, which enhances and promotes water permeability. As a result, the CDs are essential in raising the rate of plant germination. Across all concentrations, the percentage inhibition of growth (PIG) values are found to be insignificant, showing that the produced CDs are biocompatible and non-toxic with the wheat seeds employed. The Percentage Inhibition of Growth (PIG) values decreased by 42.8 % as the concentration of CDs increased from 100 to 750 ppm (Fig. 9b), indicating the supportive effect of CDs on plant growth. The results suggest that the minimal PIG values correlate with enhanced germination ability and germination index, consistent with the previously obtained results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of seed vigor-II and vigor index-I in the presence of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-proliferative activity of seeds has been greatly impacted by the unique characteristics of CDs, especially with regard to the values of the Vigor Index. In addition to germination and viability testing, seed vigour is an essential quality parameter that sheds light on seed performance. The average seedling length and germination rate have the most effects on the Vigor Index-I. The selected seedlings Vigor Index has been significantly impacted by variations in the average root length because the germination rate is relatively constant under all conditions. The maximum Vigor Index-I value is obtained to be 4990 at a concentration of 750 ppm of CDs, where the average root length peaked (Fig. 9c). More resilient and more adapted to thrive in a variety of environmental situations are the usual perceptions of seeds with higher vigor indices. The computed seed vigor-II index is shown in Fig. 9d, which is an important component of seed quality. The germination ability and the weight of the dried seedlings are the two primary factors of this measure. Given that the germination ability remained constant in every instance, it can be said that the weight of the dry seedlings (mg) had the biggest impact on the seed vigor-II score. The average weight of the seeds varied with the concentration of CDs during the germination investigation. As a result, a greater seed vigor-II (4475 at 750 ppm) correlates with a higher weight of seedlings. The findings show that at 750 ppm of CDs, both vigor index-I and seed vigor-II are optimized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Migration of CDs in wheat sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic absorption mechanism of CDs in wheat sprouts is depicted in Fig. 10 a. We saw as the CDs in the wheat sprouts migrated during the cultivation phase. Following a 10-day exposure to a 750 ppm CDs solution, when the wheat sprouts are visualized under UV light, their roots gave forth a pronounced blue glow. This suggests that the CDs are dispersed uniformly across the root system. The CDs moved from the roots to the stems and ultimately to the leaves throughout time. The CDs FL intensity is found to dramatically drop as they passed through the wheat sprouts and upward. A discernible glow developed between the plant's stem and root as we watched the wheat sprouts absorb carbon particles. During the migration phase, CDs first gather near the tips of the roots before moving towards leaves (Fig. 10b). After that, they gather at the tips of the stems before going to the leaf area. Due to significant absorption of CDs at a concentration of 750 ppm, the main stem, leaves and roots of the wheat sprouts show high FL, with the CDs aggregating in these particular locations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of wheat seeds chlorophyll content and water-uptake capacity when CDs are present
ce:section.ce:para: Plant development is significantly influenced by the amount of chlorophyll since it is inversely correlated with the rate of photosynthetic reaction, the overall quantity of electron transport and light-harvesting complexes. Plants of different kinds can have their leaves penetrated by CDs, which also improve the thylakoid membrane structure. By raising the chlorophyll content, this enhancement encourages the growth of healthier, more mature plants [90]. In this study, wheat seeds are exposed to normal light for ten days before the amount of chlorophyll is assessed. In comparison to the control group, the findings showed that the chlorophyll content peaked at 750 ppm concentration of CDs (Fig. S6a), suggesting greater photosynthetic activity because the CDs surface has more adsorption sites. The chlorophyll content is somewhat lower at 1000 ppm and much lower at 100 ppm than at 750 ppm, but it is still greater than in the control group, indicating that the created system functions well at all tested doses (Table 4 ). Reduced levels of chlorophyll are associated with altered seed development and metabolism, which can impact wheat seed germination and photosynthesis. The marginal decline in the germination index noted at a 1000 ppm concentration could be explained by the overabundance of CDs clogging seed pores, which hinders water absorption and thus germination processes [91]. The seeds that had sprouted had the maximum chlorophyll concentration (750 ppm), which is followed by the 1000 ppm, 500 ppm, 250 ppm, 100 ppm and control.

Additional examination of the moisture content and the activity of water absorption in wheat seeds indicates that the selected CDs affect the sprouted seeds cellular metabolism. Thermogravimetric analysis (weight loss) is used to determine the moisture content of dried seeds within a temperature range of 20 to 250 °C. We investigated the moisture content of seeds treated with various CDs doses (Fig. S6b). The research showed that the control dry seeds had an 18 % moisture content, whereas the seeds that are exposed to water had a 30 % moisture content. The moisture content rose in a linear fashion as the CDs concentration rose. The CDs concentration of 750 ppm showed the maximum moisture content, indicating their best germination potential. This is followed by the CDs concentrations of 1000 ppm, 500 ppm, 250 ppm, 100 ppm, water-absorbed seeds and dry seeds. This suggests that CDs improve the rate of permeability in wheat seeds, which in turn improves the process of water absorption.

The identification, detection, and extraction of FPs are of great importance in forensic investigations because to their crucial status as trace evidence. Crime scenes typically contain three different types of FPs: latent, indented, and visible FPs. Particularly challenging are LFPs, which are frequently hard to find and recognize in everyday situations. For this reason, improving and identifying LFPs is crucial to their application in case studies and court cases. According to reports, these LFPs may be improved by using CQDs as fluorescent powders or solutions [92]. To improve LFPs, fluorescent hues made of synthetic CDs were used. A LFP of an adult created using CDs is shown in Fig. 11 a. The fingerprint ridges, which were imprinted using CDs powder and adhered to aluminum foil, are clearly visible under 365 nm ultraviolet light. Important details like ridges and lines are easily visible, and the brilliant blue hue improves visibility. Fig. 11b–d shows the detailed enlarged photographs of these features. Level-I feature like loop, Level-II features like short ridges, eyes, dots, bifurcations, cross-overs, bridges, ridge endings, islands, and hooks, and Level-III features like sweat pores, creases, and scars are all plainly visible in the figure. For fingerprint analysis, these secondary and tertiary traits are essential [93]. The gray level intensity profile of the particular ridges of LFP is shown in Fig. 11e. The dynamic 3D surface plot of the fingerprint is displayed in Fig. 11f.

Because of possible dust buildup and natural deterioration over time, finding aged FPs poses serious issues in forensic investigations. This investigation looked at FPs that had been stored for up to 120 days, evaluating them every 30 days. Under 365 nm UV light, digital photographs of LFPs from day 1 to day 120 are shown in Fig. S7a, demonstrating that ridge patterns may be distinguished even after a long time. The uniformity and efficacy of the powder for identifying aged FPs are validated by the unique LFPs on an overhead projector sheet that were created using CDs powder and exposed to a high-intensity UV light. The different intensities between ridges and furrows are well-represented in the pixel profile (Fig. S7a1). The distinctive imprint left by human finger ridges, which include sweat pores close to eccrine glands, is mostly made up of water (>99 %), minerals (∼0.5 %), and organic materials (∼0.5 %), including sugars, amino acids, lactic acids, uric acids, chlorine, urea, and other compounds [94]. Water progressively evaporates from the ridge surface over time, producing recognizable oily residues that might complicate the identification of ridge patterns. Nonetheless, this study shows that, in typical environmental settings, finger ridges are still easily visible 120 days later. Confirmation of the repeatability and dependability of CDs for LFP detection was achieved through systematic testing. When analyzing LFPs dusted with CDs, high temperatures and extended UV exposure are important aspects that must be taken into account. This is particularly relevant when working with evidence that has been exposed to a variety of environmental circumstances. As seen in Fig. S7b, the stability of LFPs created by CDs on a marble surface was investigated in the current work at four different temperature levels: 25, 50, 100, and 250 °C. The findings show that the luminous LFPs are resistant to temperatures as high as 250 °C. The blue levels in each location vary, as seen in Fig. S7b1, with higher blue values in ridge areas and lower values in furrow regions. Furthermore, the testing of LFPs during UV irradiation using 395 nm light is shown in Fig. S7c. After being exposed to UV light for a range of times (0 to 120 min), the LFPs showed very little variation, even after 120 min of continuous exposure. The results, as depicted in the figure, suggest that the produced LFPs are not impacted by the radiation effect and only experience minor modifications. Fig. S7c1 shows the related gray value plots. These results demonstrate the products promising potential for LFP detection and imply that the synthesized CDs are ideal for applications involving LFP visualization in a variety of ecological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The distinction between the powder dusting and spray methods for identifying LFPs
ce:section.ce:para: LFPs were successfully identified on a variety of surfaces by using the optimal CDs solution for the spraying technique. Without the need for extra processing, Fig. 12 a–d provides detailed blue FL images of LFPs developed by spray method on leaf, aluminum foil, compact discs, and steel plates under 365 nm UV light. On the other hand, under 365 nm UV light, the LFPs on the same surfaces—leaf, aluminum foil, compact discs, and steel plates—were clearly visible when the CDs powder was employed for the powder dusting procedure (Fig. 12e–h). Since CDs are easily able to penetrate and react with fingerprint residues, the enhanced selectivity and specific adhesion of the CDs solution is highlighted by the obvious visibility of fingerprint structures on porous surfaces. High-luminance blue ridges are produced when the CDs powder consistently sticks to LFPs on non-porous surfaces. It has a special affinity for the ridges rather than the furrows. The ridges have a strong interaction that improves the clarity of the LFP images significantly. Blue fluorescent light has enough intensity to withstand interference from ambient light, such as UV light reflections on glossy coverings or non-porous surfaces. For LFP identification to be successful on semi-porous surfaces, two factors are needed: the physical adhesion of the CDs powder and the chemical interaction between the CDs solution and the fingerprint residues, which permits the CDs solution to diffuse. The LFPs found by both techniques are classified according to the structural characteristics of fingerprint ridges (Fig. 12i & j). The enlarged pictures show intricate structure at different levels: Level I is the core and delta, Level II is the cross-over, bifurcation, and eye and Level III is the sweat pores. But particulars underscore the significance of sweat pores in identifying individuals, even from flawed or fragmented prints, and are critical for fingerprint recognition on an individual basis. Based on features retrieved from the LFP images, the LFPs created using the spray approach exhibit a consistent alignment of nanoparticles (NPs) along the ridges (Fig. 12i). However, it was unable to effectively capture the Level III characteristics, such as sweat pores. In contrast, the features extracted from the powder-dusted LFP image demonstrate the high-resolution capability of LFP detection under UV light (Fig. 12j). Even secondary and tertiary features of the LFP are imaged with high clarity. This alignment indicates a strong binding of CDs powder to fingerprint residues. Analyzing the pixel value fluctuations in the selected regions (white strip) in Fig. 12k and l reveals the grooves and ridges with attached CDs, with the highest gray value observed for powder-dusted LFPs. The 3D surface plot supports the findings (Fig. 12m & n). This confirms the high-resolution capability of LFP detection using CDs powder.

In summary, we have successfully prepared the coal derived CDs by employing ultrasonication method. Structural characterization through XRD, Raman and XPS confirmed the composition and properties of the CDs. CDs can be well dispersed in the aqueous solution and own a blue emission of 453 nm under a 350 nm excitation (relative QY = 40.6 %). The current work revealed the mechanism of selective sensing for Fe3+ (LOD is 0.204 μM according to the 3σ rule), improving the synthesis of CDs and perfecting the detection of heavy metal ions. Furthermore, the CDs act as a promising nanoprobe for detection of Fe3+ and AA. The dynamic quenching, evidenced by the reduction in average lifetime. The quenched emission of the CDs is subsequently restored upon the addition of AA through a redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA. Additionally, a multi-input molecular logic gate is constructed based on the obtained FL results, paving the way for the development of FL-based smart molecular devices. In addition, using the developed nano sensor for the fluorometric detection of TC, within a linear range of 5–50 μM, we are able to attain an outstanding LoD of 0.385 μM, accompanied by a notable FL color change from bright blue to yellow. The results demonstrated positive effects of seed priming at all CDs concentrations (100–1000 ppm), including accelerated seed germination rate, increased shoot and root elongation, biomass accumulation and root moisture level compared to the control groups. CDs (750 ppm) effectively enhanced seed germination, with the migration mechanism of CDs in wheat sprouts confirmed. This supports their potential as a nano priming agent to improve seedling growth in agricultural applications. LFP analysis using CDs allows us to examine the minute level III features. Using spray and powder dusting techniques on LFPs across various substrates reveals distinct fluorescent patterns when exposed to 365 nm UV light, maintaining stability across diverse conditions. The obtained findings have suggested that the developed CDs have possessed outstanding applications in forensic and agricultural field. The information's achieved herein will allow the usage of CDs as a capable nano priming agent for natural germination of seeds and enhanced their agronomical paradigm, and highly effective in personal identifications.
